title: SIEM Alert-to-Incident Ratio Trending
dataSources: `index=notable`, disposition fields (`status`, `urgency`, `owner`), optional `index=itsi` if incidents sync externally
spl:
index=notable earliest=-90d
| eval confirmed=if(match(status,"Closed - True Positive"),1,0)
| eval triaged_incident=if(match(status,"(New|In Progress|Pending)") OR match(owner,"SOC|Incident"),1,0)
| bin _time span=1w
| stats count as alerts sum(confirmed) as true_positives sum(triaged_incident) as in_flight by _time
| eval tp_rate_pct=round(100*true_positives/nullif(alerts,0),2)
| timechart span=1w first(alerts) as weekly_alerts first(tp_rate_pct) as true_positive_rate_pct
description: Comparing ES notables to analyst-confirmed incidents shows whether the SOC is drowning in noise or under-escalating real cases. A falling ratio over 90 days may mean tuning drift; a rising ratio can indicate improved correlation or staffing effects worth validating.
value: Comparing ES notables to analyst-confirmed incidents shows whether the SOC is drowning in noise or under-escalating real cases. A falling ratio over 90 days may mean tuning drift; a rising ratio can indicate improved correlation or staffing effects worth validating.
implementation: Align `status` values with your ES workflow (Closed - True Positive / False Positive). If you use formal incident tickets, join notables to `incident_id` from a lookup populated by SOAR or ITSM export. Review weekly with detection engineering to separate tuning opportunities from genuine threat volume changes. Exclude test rules via `rule_name` lookup.
visualization: Dual-axis line chart (alerts vs true positives), column chart (weekly alert volume), single value (90-day TP rate trend).
knownFalsePositives: The ratio of alerts to formal incidents changes when we tighten ticket discipline, on-board a SOAR, or reclassify noise — it is a process signal as much as a threat signal.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=notable`, disposition fields (`status`, `urgency`, `owner`), optional `index=itsi` if incidents sync externally.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Align `status` values with your ES workflow (Closed - True Positive / False Positive). If you use formal incident tickets, join notables to `incident_id` from a lookup populated by SOAR or ITSM export. Review weekly with detection engineering to separate tuning opportunities from genuine threat volume changes. Exclude test rules via `rule_name` lookup.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=notable earliest=-90d
| eval confirmed=if(match(status,"Closed - True Positive"),1,0)
| eval triaged_incident=if(match(status,"(New|In Progress|Pending)") OR match(owner,"SOC|Incident"),1,0)
| bin _time span=1w
| stats count as alerts sum(confirmed) as true_positives sum(triaged_incident) as in_flight by _time
| eval tp_rate_pct=round(100*true_positives/nullif(alerts,0),2)
| timechart span=1w first(alerts) as weekly_alerts first(tp_rate_pct) as true_positive_rate_pct
```

Understanding this SPL

**SIEM Alert-to-Incident Ratio Trending** — Comparing ES notables to analyst-confirmed incidents shows whether the SOC is drowning in noise or under-escalating real cases. A falling ratio over 90 days may mean tuning drift; a rising ratio can indicate improved correlation or staffing effects worth validating.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=notable`, disposition fields (`status`, `urgency`, `owner`), optional `index=itsi` if incidents sync externally. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: notable.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=notable, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **confirmed** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **triaged_incident** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **tp_rate_pct** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1w** buckets — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.

Step 3 — Validate
Reconcile a week’s notables in **ES Incident Review** to your incident ticketing export so the ratio in Splunk matches the ticket system’s story.



Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Dual-axis line chart (alerts vs true positives), column chart (weekly alert volume), single value (90-day TP rate trend).
grandmaExplanation: We watch how many raw alerts turn into real tracked incidents so we know whether we are just noisy or actually closing the loop.
